perturbation.original_text: 4. Disclosure Period and Termin ation. This Agreement controls Confidential Informati on which is disclosed from the Effective D ate until terminate d. Recipient’s duty of non -disclosure under this Agreement shall extend beyond the term of thi s Agreement for a period of three (3) years from the date of last disclosure. This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other party. The parties agree that Confidential Information constituting a trade se cret shall remain subject to the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement for so long as such Confidential Information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Disclosure Period and Termin ation. This Agreement controls Confidential Informati on which is disclosed from the Effective D ate until terminate d. Recipient’s duty of non -disclosure under this Agreement shall extend beyond the term of thi s Agreement for a period of one (1) year from the date of last disclosure. This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other party. The parties agree that Confidential Information constituting a trade se cret shall remain subject to the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement for so long as such Confidential Information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: The change reduces the non-disclosure period from three years to one year. This introduces a contradiction because, while trade secrets are protected for as long as they qualify as such, other confidential information now has a significantly shorter protection window. This creates uncertainty in enforcement regarding information that doesn't meet the strict definition of a trade secret, but was intended to be kept confidential for three years as the original text stated. This directly contradicts with Section 2's definition, encompassing data with continuous business value, where a one-year limitation could be insufficient, creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Confidential Information Defined . “Confidential Information” shall mean and include (i) samples and prototypes, (ii) all information disclosed in any form or medium (e.g., written, verbal, electro nic, visual) which (a) has been identified by Sanmina as “Confidential,” “Proprietary” or other appropriate legend indicating the confidential nature of the information or (b) would reasonably be understood to be confidential. Confidential Information inc ludes, but is not limited to, information regarding pricing, customers and prospective customers, vendors and vendor lists, costed bills of materials, processes (including but not limited to manufacturing processes), know -how, designs (including but not li mited to designs of enclosures and printed circuit boards), formulae, computer programs, databases, methods of operation, sales techniques, business methods or plans, marketing plans and strategies, finances, management, plant and equipment, and any other business information relating to Sanmina, whether constituting a trade secret, proprietary information or otherwise, which has value to Sanmina and is treated by Sanmina as being confidential .
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a three-year non-disclosure period, while the changed text reduces this to one year. Section 2 defines 'Confidential Information' broadly, encompassing data with continuous business value. The reduction to a one-year protection for non-trade secret confidential information directly contradicts the intent of Section 2, as a one-year limitation may be insufficient to protect the business value of the information, thus creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Return of Confidential Information. Within ten (10) days after any written request by Sanmina, the Recipient shall promptly return all copies of the Confidential Information except copies required for backup, disaster recovery, or business continuity and in such case the obligations hereunder shall survive until such copies are destroyed .
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Return of Confidential Information. Within thirty (30) days after any written request by Sanmina, the Recipient shall use commercially reasonable efforts to return all copies of the Confidential Information except copies required for backup, disaster recovery, or business continuity and in such case the obligations hereunder shall only survive for 90 days.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return timeframe from 'ten (10) days' to 'thirty (30) days' and the explicit obligation of immediate destruction, makes it difficult to ensure total compliance with Section 5 'Duty to Protect' because the text is not implying the recipient to use reasonable care, but an explicit duty. This creates an in-text contradiction on the level of care being granted to the information, and the duration of protection implied in Section 5, particularly given the potential value of Confidential Information as detailed in Section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Duty to Protect. Recipient shall protect the disclosed Confidential Informat ion by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Confidential Information as the Recipient uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature. Recipient shall (i) 2 NDA Rev. 3 -14-14 Form NDA -3 (Supplier) limit access to all Confidential Information to its employees who shall reasonably require access to the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth above, (ii) shall insure that all such employees have signed a nondisc losure agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement, (iii) shall use the Confidential Information solely in connection with the Purpose, and (iv) shall use due diligence to insure that all such persons comply with the provisions of this Agr eement. Recipient shall be liable for any breach of this Agreement by its employees or representatives.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by changing the return timeframe of confidential information from 'ten (10) days' to 'thirty (30) days' and limiting the survival of obligations to '90 days.' This directly conflicts with Section 5, 'Duty to Protect,' which requires the recipient to use the 'same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care' to protect the confidential information. The extended return timeframe and limited survival of obligations make it more difficult to ensure compliance with the 'Duty to Protect,' creating a clear and impactful contradiction regarding the level of care required and the duration of that protection.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to its conflicts of law princip les. The parties agree that the state courts of Santa Clara County, California and the federal courts located in the Northern District of the State of California shall have exclusive jurisdiction and venue to adjudicate any and all disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement. The parties consent to the exercise by such courts of personal jurisdiction over them and each party waives any objection it might otherwise have to venue, personal jurisdiction, inconvenience of forum, and any similar or related doctrine.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflicts of law princip les. The parties agree that the state courts of Santa Clara County, California and the federal courts located in the Northern District of the State of California shall have exclusive jurisdiction and venue to adjudicate any and all disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement. The parties consent to the exercise by such courts of personal jurisdiction over them and each party waives any objection it might otherwise have to venue, personal jurisdiction, inconvenience of forum, and any similar or related doctrine.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Delaware while retaining California courts for jurisdiction creates a conflict. Delaware law may interpret confidentiality obligations differently than California law. This directly affects Section 10 'Remedies', because the availability and nature of injunctive relief, as well as the interpretation of 'irreparable injury', depend on the applicable governing law. Thus, an in-text contradiction arises, as the contract specifies remedies under one legal framework while obligating the court to apply a different one.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. Remedies. Recipient agrees that its obligations here under are necessary and reasonable in order to protect Sanmina and Sanmina’s business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages will be inadequate to compensate Sanmina for any breach by either party of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, Recipient agrees and acknowledges that any such viola tion or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to Sanmina and that, in addition to any other remedies th at may be avail able, in law, in equity or otherwise, Sanmina will be entitled to seek injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages and without the necessity of posting bond.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from California to Delaware, while the contradicted text (Section 10) outlines remedies and bases them on assumptions suitable for Sanmina and California businesses. Delaware law may interpret confidentiality obligations differently than California law. As the explanation outlines, the change creates an in-text contradiction as the contract now specifies remedies under California expectations while requiring the court (in California) to apply Delaware law. Therefore, the changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text, as the remedies outlined might not be valid or enforceable under Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: 12. Entire Agreement. This document contains the complete and exclusive Agreement between the parties, and it is intended to be final expression of their agreement. No promise, representation, warranty or covenant not included in this document has been or is relied upon by any party. Each party has relied upon its own examination of the warranties, representations and covenants expressly contained in the Agreement itself. No modification or amendment of this Agreement shall be o f any force unless in writing executed by all parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Entire Agreement. This document contains the complete Agreement between the parties. No promise, representation, warranty or covenant not included in this document has been or is relied upon by any party, except for prior agreements regarding similar confidential information. Each party has relied upon its own examination of the warranties, representations and covenants expressly contained in the Agreement itself. No modification or amendment of this Agreement shall be of any force unless acknowledged by email from both parties.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the Entire Agreement clause to allow reliance on prior agreements and acknowledge amendments via email creates an ambiguity and contradiction. It contradicts the original intent of the clause, which was to make the current document the *exclusive* and *final* expression of the agreement. Now, prior agreements could be considered, creating confusion about the scope of Confidential Information defined in Section 2. Also the writting requirement from Section 12 is now being overruled by an email being sent, creating contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Confidential Information Defined . “Confidential Information” shall mean and include (i) samples and prototypes, (ii) all information disclosed in any form or medium (e.g., written, verbal, electro nic, visual) which (a) has been identified by Sanmina as “Confidential,” “Proprietary” or other appropriate legend indicating the confidential nature of the information or (b) would reasonably be understood to be confidential. Confidential Information inc ludes, but is not limited to, information regarding pricing, customers and prospective customers, vendors and vendor lists, costed bills of materials, processes (including but not limited to manufacturing processes), know -how, designs (including but not li mited to designs of enclosures and printed circuit boards), formulae, computer programs, databases, methods of operation, sales techniques, business methods or plans, marketing plans and strategies, finances, management, plant and equipment, and any other business information relating to Sanmina, whether constituting a trade secret, proprietary information or otherwise, which has value to Sanmina and is treated by Sanmina as being confidential .
perturbation.justification: The original Entire Agreement clause aimed for exclusivity and finality. The changed text now allows reliance on prior agreements regarding confidential information, which directly contradicts the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 2, as it introduces uncertainty about which agreements define the scope of confidential information. Also, modifying the amendment process from 'written execution by all parties' to 'acknowledgment by email' directly contradicts the original clause's explicit requirement, creating a strong in-text contradiction. This means the original condition to modifications and changes in the contract are no longer the same, hence creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Notices . Wherever one party is required or permitted or required to give written notice to the other under this Agreement, such notice will be given by hand, by certified U.S. mail, return rec eipt requested, by overnight courier, or by fax and addressed to the appropriate officer of such party (with a copy to the Legal Department) at the address set forth in the preamble. All such notices shall be effective upon receipt. Either party may desi gnate a different notice address from time to time upon giving five (5) days’ prior written notice thereof to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Notices . Wherever one party is required or permitted or required to give written notice to the other under this Agreement, such notice will be given by email (without requiring confirmation of receipt), by certified U.S. mail, return rec eipt requested, by overnight courier, or by fax and addressed to the appropriate officer of such party (with a copy to the Legal Department) at the address set forth in the preamble. All such notices shall be effective upon sending. Either party may desi gnate a different notice address from time to time upon giving five (5) days’ prior verbal notice thereof to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing notice via email without requiring confirmation and changing the effective date to 'upon sending' introduces ambiguity and contradiction regarding the termination clause in Section 4. Previously, 'thirty (30) days written notice' implied receipt and verifiable confirmation. Now, a termination email could be sent, and considered effective even if never received, or challenged in court, creating uncertainty. The verbal notice for the designation of a different notice address, is directly contradicting the definition of written notice, from the first sentense of Section 13.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Disclosure Period and Termin ation. This Agreement controls Confidential Informati on which is disclosed from the Effective D ate until terminate d. Recipient’s duty of non -disclosure under this Agreement shall extend beyond the term of thi s Agreement for a period of three (3) years from the date of last disclosure. This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other party. The parties agree that Confidential Information constituting a trade se cret shall remain subject to the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement for so long as such Confidential Information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces two contradictions. First, it allows notice via email without requiring confirmation, and changes the effective date of the notice to 'upon sending.' This contradicts Section 4, which requires 'thirty (30) days written notice' for termination, as written notice previously implied receipt and verifiable confirmation. Now, a termination email could be sent but never received, yet still be considered effective, creating uncertainty and conflicting with the original intent of a guaranteed 30-day notice period. Second, the change from 'written notice' to 'verbal notice' for designating a different notice address directly contradicts the requirement for written notice as stated in the first sentence of Section 13, creating an ambiguity and rendering the initial definition of 'written notice' inconsistent.

perturbation.original_text: 14. Assignment . Neither this Agreement nor any rights or obligations hereunder shall be transferred or assigned by either party without the written consent of the other party, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld or delayed. Sanmina Corporation __________________________________ By:______________________________ By:_______________________________ Printed Name:______________________ Printed Name:______________________ Title:_____________________________ Title:_____________________________ Date:_____________________________ Date:_____________________________
perturbation.changed_text: 14. Assignment . Neither this Agreement nor any rights or obligations hereunder shall be transferred or assigned by either party. Sanmina Corporation can assign this agreement freely, without prior notice. __________________________________ By:______________________________ By:_______________________________ Printed Name:______________________ Printed Name:______________________ Title:_____________________________ Title:_____________________________ Date:_____________________________ Date:_____________________________
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for written consent for assignment and granting Sanmina the right to assign the agreement freely creates a contradiction with Section 5 'Duty to Protect'. If Sanmina assigns the agreement to a third party with lax security measures, the Recipient's duty to protect Sanmina's confidential information might be compromised. This creates uncertainty as to whether Recipient must now adhere to the security standards of the assignee, even if they are lower than what's considered 'reasonable care' under Section 5, introducing an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Duty to Protect. Recipient shall protect the disclosed Confidential Informat ion by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Confidential Information as the Recipient uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature. Recipient shall (i) 2 NDA Rev. 3 -14-14 Form NDA -3 (Supplier) limit access to all Confidential Information to its employees who shall reasonably require access to the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth above, (ii) shall insure that all such employees have signed a nondisc losure agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement, (iii) shall use the Confidential Information solely in connection with the Purpose, and (iv) shall use due diligence to insure that all such persons comply with the provisions of this Agr eement. Recipient shall be liable for any breach of this Agreement by its employees or representatives.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for written consent for assignment by Sanmina, allowing them to assign the agreement freely. This contradicts Section 5, which places a 'Duty to Protect' Sanmina's confidential information on the Recipient. If Sanmina assigns the agreement to a third party with weaker security measures, the Recipient's ability to protect the information to the standard of 'reasonable care' is compromised. This creates an in-text contradiction because the Recipient's obligations under Section 5 are now unclear, potentially conflicting with the assignee's standards, making them incompatible.
